Motivation: The rapid evolution of Quantum Development Kits (QDKs) creates a form of technical debt, complicating maintainability and software reuse in QSE, where scarce training data and volatile frameworks exacerbate these challenges.

Method: The authors integrate LLMs with RAG guided by an automatically generated taxonomy of migration scenarios to provide precise and reliable migration suggestions, using an automated workflow to evaluate LLMs under different retrieval schemes.

Result: The taxonomy-based RAG architecture improved reliability, with the restrictive retrieval scheme reducing hallucinations and Google Gemini Flash-2.5 performing best, especially for complex refactoring tasks.

Conclusion: This data-centric approach demonstrates potential for mitigating API obsolescence and supporting the long-term usability of quantum algorithms within the rapidly evolving QSE ecosystem.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of Quantum Development Kits (QDKs) introduces a specific form of technical debt that compromises code maintainability and hinders software reuse. In the specialized domain of Quantum Software Engineering (QSE), this challenge is intensified by the scarcity of high-quality training data and the high volatility of emerging frameworks, which often lead general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) to produce unreliable or hallucinated results. This paper proposes a hybrid approach integrating LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to automate the migration of Qiskit code across versions. The proposed methodology enhances the precision and reliability of migration suggestions by leveraging an automatically generated taxonomy of migration scenarios as the structured, version-specific knowledge source to guide the models. The approach is implemented through an automated, extensible workflow evaluating LLMs (Google Gemini Flash-2.5 and OpenAI Gpt-oss-20b) under different retrieval schemes (unconstrained and restrictive). Results demonstrate that the taxonomy-based RAG architecture, particularly under the restrictive scheme, significantly reduces hallucinations and improves descriptive quality, with Google Gemini Flash-2.5 showing superior performance in detecting complex refactoring scenarios. These findings confirm the potential of this data-centric methodology to foster technological independence and provide robust, intelligent assistants that mitigate API obsolescence, ensuring the long-term availability of quantum algorithms within a rapidly shifting ecosystem and flattening the learning curve within Quantum Software Engineering (QSE).

</details>


### [463] [SysML Modeling of Digital Twins for Renewable Energy Communities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20230)
*Mohammad Samadi,Luís Miguel Pinho,Andrey Sadovykh,Gabriela Lucas*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a model-based approach for creating Digital Twins of Renewable Energy Communities using SysML and explores addressing semantic gaps with the SAREF4ENER ontology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address challenges in engineering Digital Twins for Renewable Energy Communities, arising due to device, contract, and data heterogeneity.

Method: A Model-Based Systems Engineering workflow is proposed using SysML and the open-source tool Modelio, with a focus on device taxonomy and community organization in RECs.

Result: Two Block Definition Diagrams representing device taxonomy and community organizational views are developed while identifying semantic gaps in plain SysML.

Conclusion: The combination of SysML and SAREF-based semantics for smart-energy Digital Twins is proposed as a promising approach, marking this paper as an initial step in that direction.

Abstract: Renewable Energy Communities (RECs) are emerging as a key organizational model for local and global sharing of renewable generation, storage, and flexible loads. Engineering Digital Twins of RECs is made difficult by the heterogeneity of devices, contracts, and runtime data involved. In this paper, we take a first step toward a Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) workflow for REC's Digital Twins. Starting from an industrially-validated REC domain model, we re-express a representative house subset in SysML using the open-source Modelio tool, yielding two Block Definition Diagrams - a device taxonomy and a community organizational view. We then discuss four semantic gaps that plain SysML leaves open and sketch how the SAREF4ENER ontology could be imported as a reference package to close them. Combining SysML with SAREF-based semantics for smart-energy Digital Twins remains largely unexplored, and we position this paper as a first step along that line.

</details>


### [464] [Phoenix: Safe GitHub Issue Resolution via Multi-Agent LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20243)
*Kipngeno Koech,Muhammad Adam,Baimam Boukar Jean Jacques,Joao Barros*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Phoenix is a multi-agent system for resolving GitHub issues through pull-request creation, containing layered safety controls and using a test evaluation strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve GitHub issue resolution workflow by leveraging specialized agents and automated processes.

Method: Six agents handle distinct tasks: planning, reproduction, coding, testing, analysis, and pull request formation, coordinated through GitHub webhook state machines.

Result: On curated SWE-bench Lite instances, Phoenix resolves 75% of issues without test regressions; pilot tests show 100% correctness but a 50% rate of misplaced fixes.

Conclusion: Phoenix demonstrates promise in automating issue resolution but requires enhancements in code placement accuracy and handling deployment failures.

Abstract: We present Phoenix, a multi-agent LLM system that resolves GitHub issues from triage through pull-request creation, combining seven layered safety controls with a baseline-aware test evaluation strategy. Phoenix decomposes the work across six specialized agents. Planner, reproducer, coder, tester, failure analyst and Pull Request (PR) agent, all coordinated by a label-based GitHub webhook state machine. Every change is checked against a baseline test run before a pull request is opened. On a 24-instance slice of SWE-bench Lite. run on the production webhook path, Phoenix oracle-resolves 75% of instances with no pass-to-pass regressions on successful runs; this curated slice is not directly comparable to full-split leaderboard results, and we discuss the limits of the comparison. A complementary pilot on 42 real issues across 14 repositories yields 100% correctness preservation (CP; mean 122s on the hard tier). Manual inspection shows that about half of the resulting pull requests are well-targeted fixes. The other half place code at incorrect paths, a planner localization limitation we are addressing with retrieval. We also report the deployment failure modes (WAF filtering, token expiry, permission boundaries, flaky CI) that motivated each safety mechanism.

</details>


### [465] [Token-Operations-Oriented Inference Optimization Techniques for Large Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20295)
*Shiguo Lian,Kai Wang,Zhaoxiang Liu,Wen Liu,Minjie Hua,Yutong Liu,Jiangze Yan,Xin Wang,Cong Wang,Yilin Zhang,Yi Shen,Jieyun Huang,Fang Zhao,Huanlin Gao,Ping Chen,Xinyu Yang,Kaikai Zhao,Yao Zhao,Xinggang Wang,Huishuai Zhang,Dongyan Zhao,Junping Du,Tao Chen,Xiang Gao,Qinghuai Ma*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a four-layer framework for optimizing large model inference to enhance efficiency, reduce costs, and improve stability in delivering large model services.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges of scalability, cost-efficiency, and stability in operating large model services, focusing on token-oriented inference optimization.

Method: The authors present a four-layer architecture: Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, and Compute-Network-Model Fusion. They systematically review technologies and industry trends within these layers.

Result: The study highlights the practical value of these technologies in real-world settings, which include reducing costs per token, enhancing token service efficiency, and improving operational stability.

Conclusion: The paper offers a roadmap for transitioning large model services from being callable to fully operable systems by employing systematic technological optimizations.

Abstract: Large model inference optimization serves as a key foundation for supporting the scalable, low-cost, and highly stable operation of large model services. Centered on token-oriented inference optimization technology, this paper proposes for the first time a four-layer technical architecture consisting of Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, and Compute-Network-Model Fusion. It systematically reviews the key technologies and current industry status across these four levels and analyzes the application value of related technologies in real-world business scenarios. This paper provides a practical technical path for reducing token production costs, improving token service efficiency, ensuring the stability of token supply, and driving the transition of large model services from being merely callable to being operable.

</details>


### [466] [A Model-Driven Approach for Developing Families of Reinforcement Learning Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20324)
*Xiaoran Liu,Istvan David*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a model-driven approach and a prototype tool for generating families of reinforcement learning (RL) training environments to replace labor-intensive manual development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of labor-intensive and error-prone manual development of environment families for RL training, which is necessary for solving realistic RL problems involving slightly varying environments.

Method: A hybrid genetic algorithm combining global population-based search and heuristic local search is used, along with model transformations for generating environment families. A state-of-the-art model transformation engine operationalizes the process.

Result: The soundness of the approach and prototype tool is demonstrated through experiments in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning.

Conclusion: The model-driven approach effectively creates RL training environment families, offering a scalable, automated alternative to manual development methods.

Abstract: Virtual training environments are software-intensive systems in which reinforcement learning (RL) agents learn, adapt, and demonstrate meaningful behavior. Virtual training environments offer a safe and cost-efficient alternative to training agents in real-world settings. However, to converge, most realistic RL problems require training in multiple, mostly similar but slightly different environments - i.e., families of environment variants. The typical development process of environment families is a labor-intensive and error-prone manual endeavor that does not scale well. To alleviate these issues, in this paper, we propose a model-driven approach for developing families of RL training environments. To obtain the family of environments, we develop an approach and prototype tool. In our approach, a hybrid genetic algorithm - a combination of population-based global search and heuristic local search - generates environment families. Mutations and constraints are expressed as model transformations and are operationalized into a search process by a state-of-the-art model transformation engine. We demonstrate the soundness of our approach in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning - a particular learning paradigm that relies on environment families.

</details>


### [467] [AutoPass: Evidence-Guided LLM Agents for Compiler Performance Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20373)
*Zepeng Li,Jie Ren,Zhanyong Tang,Jie Zheng,Zheng Wang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: AutoPass introduces a multi-agent framework leveraging LLMs for compiler performance tuning, achieving notable runtime speedups on x86-64 and ARM64 systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in runtime performance tuning caused by complex microarchitectural effects and noisy runtime measurements, and to improve compiler optimization processes.

Method: AutoPass uses a multi-agent system where LLMs guide optimization decisions by collaborating with the compiler. It involves querying compiler-internal states, analyzing intermediate representations, and iteratively refining compiler configurations based on runtime feedback, all without the need for training or fine-tuning.

Result: AutoPass achieved speedups of 1.043x on x86-64 and 1.117x on ARM64 systems compared to LLVM -O3 optimizations, outperforming expert-tuned and classical autotuning methods.

Conclusion: AutoPass demonstrates that integrating LLMs with compilers opens new pathways for improved optimization, making performance tuning more effective and broadly applicable without requiring task-specific pretraining.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for code compilation tasks, but applying them to runtime performance tuning is difficult due to complex microarchitectural effects and noisy runtime measurements. We present AutoPass, a multi-agent framework for compiler performance tuning that uses compiler and runtime evidence to guide LLM-generated optimization decisions. Rather than treating the compiler as a black box like prior auto-tuning schemes, AutoPass opens up the compiler to the LLM, enabling it to query compiler-internal optimization states and analyze the intermediate representation to orchestrate compiler options. The search process iteratively refines optimization configurations using measured runtime feedback to diagnose regressions and guide latency-improving edits. AutoPass operates in an inference-only, training-free setting and requires no offline training or task-specific fine-tuning, making it readily applicable to new benchmarks and platforms. We implement AutoPass on the LLVM compiler and evaluate it on server-grade x86-64 and embedded ARM64 systems. AutoPass outperforms expert-tuned heuristics and classical autotuning methods, achieving geometric-mean speedups of 1.043x and 1.117x over LLVM -O3 on x86-64 and ARM64, respectively.

</details>


### [468] [Probe-and-Refine Tuning of Repository Guidance for Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20512)
*Asa Shepard,Jeannie Albrecht*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method, "probe-and-refine tuning," to improve LLM-based coding agents' ability to utilize repository guidance files for better performance. Evaluations demonstrate significant performance gains when synthetic bug-fix probes are used iteratively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the issue of limited operational knowledge in LLM-based coding agents, arguing that the existing context provided (e.g., AGENTS.md files) often fails due to ineffective guidance generation.

Method: The authors introduce "probe-and-refine tuning," an iterative method that uses synthetic bug-fix probes to diagnose and refine guidance files in a repository. This process avoids agent loops or tool use during tuning, relying solely on single-shot LLM calls.

Result: Testing shows that "probe-and-refine tuning" achieved a 33.0% mean resolve rate, better than static knowledge bases (28.3%) and unguided baselines (25.5%). Improvements stem from broader coverage as agents locate files more effectively rather than improving patch precision directly.

Conclusion: Refined guidance enhances LLM-based coding agents' ability to navigate repositories and utilize step budgets, improving operational capabilities. However, effectiveness depends on the model's ability to produce diagnostic outputs.

Abstract: LLM-based coding agents need higher-level operational knowledge about a repository (which files house which subsystems, how to run the test suite, which workflows have historically led to wrong fixes) that does not exist in the code itself. Engineers typically maintain \texttt{AGENTS.md} files to supply this context as instructions for coding agents, but whether they help is contested: recent studies disagree on whether LLM-generated guidance improves or harms agent performance. In this paper we show that how the guidance is produced is the decisive variable, and introduce \emph{probe-and-refine tuning}: a procedure that uses synthetic bug-fix probes to iteratively diagnose and patch a repository's guidance file through single-shot LLM calls, with no agent loop or tool use during tuning. On SWE-bench Verified across four independent trials with Qwen3.5-35B-A3B at 200 steps, probe-and-refine achieves 33.0\,\% mean resolve rate vs.\ 28.3\,\% for the static knowledge base used to initialize it and 25.5\,\% for an unguided baseline ($p < 0.001$ for both probe-and-refine contrasts). The improvement comes from coverage rather than precision: refined guidance produces evaluable patches for 14.5 percentage points (pp) more instances while per-patch precision remains statistically constant ($\sim$59\,\%, $p = 0.119$), showing that improved guidance helps agents reach the correct file rather than improving the quality of the changes they make. Further, a step-budget experiment shows that guidance is what lets the agent use a larger step budget productively, and a cross-model experiment with NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B finds that the tuning loop degrades when the model cannot generate sufficiently diagnostic output, though per-patch precision remains constant even then.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [469] [Robust probabilistic measurement of structural-functional module consistency in infant brain development](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19739)
*Lingbin Bian,Feihong Liu,Qian Wang,Han Zhang,Dinggang Shen,the UNC/UMN Baby Connectome Project Consortium*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper introduces stochastic modules within brain networks to robustly measure structural-functional module consistency (SFMC) across individuals, showcasing developmental trends and regional differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of evaluating consistency between structural and functional brain modules across individuals, especially with inter-individual variability and varying module sizes.

Method: The study utilizes stochastic modules to compute assignment probabilities of brain regions to group-level sub-networks and compares structural-functional module consistency using these probabilistic measures.

Result: The analysis reveals that SFMC declines from 0 to 5 years of age, with higher values in primary brain regions (e.g., visual areas) and lower values in advanced cognitive regions (e.g., attention and default mode network).

Conclusion: Stochastic module-based measurement provides a robust framework for capturing developmental brain network reorganization, emphasizing structural-functional integration and variability across age groups and regions in the BCP dataset.

Abstract: Brain network is commonly divided into modules for analyzing their functionally segregated roles for group-level analysis in neuroimaging studies. Here, we introduce stochastic modules within brain networks for a robust probabilistic measurement of structural-functional module consistency (SFMC) in a group of subjects. Specifically, a stochastic module can be regarded as the chance of a brain region across subjects potentially being assigned to a group-level sub-network, characterized as an assignment probability for this brain region. This novel method has two advantages for evaluating inhomogeneous modules in brain networks. The first is that it can robustly evaluate the consistency between brain structural and functional modules whose population sizes are not necessary the same, and the second is that it is able to take into account the inter-individual variability of the modules for the groups. Moreover, compared with the conventional structural-functional coupling approach, our stochastic module-based method reveals a more pronounced decline in the coupling between structure and function, indicating stronger developmental reorganization. Our results using the dataset from Baby Connectome Project (BCP) show that the SFMC decreases from 0 to 5 years old, and is greater in primary brain regions, such as visual areas, while lower in more advanced cognitive regions, including those related to attention, control, and default mode network.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [470] [The Representational Limit of Scalar Interactions: An Interventional Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19410)
*Potito Aghilar,Sabino Roccotelli,Stanislao Fidanza,Vito Walter Anelli,Sebastiano Stramaglia,Tommaso Di Noia*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper addresses the conflation of uniqueness (U), redundancy (R), and synergy (S) in signed pairwise interaction scores and introduces Stochastic Hi-Fi to separate these components effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing interaction scores fail to distinguish between uniqueness, redundancy, and synergy in pairwise interactions, leading to a fundamental conflation of these attributes.

Method: The study proposes Stochastic Hi-Fi, a novel decomposition approach that provides per-feature U/R/S profiles using interventional masked inference and features such as Monte Carlo bounds and variance reduction.

Result: Stochastic Hi-Fi effectively recovers overlooked structural interactions, separates redundant and synergistic components within complex models, and demonstrates superior results in tasks like ChestX-ray14 analysis over baseline methods.

Conclusion: Stochastic Hi-Fi represents an advanced method for disentangling interaction types (U/R/S), solving limitations in traditional models while showing significant performance gains in practical applications.

Abstract: Signed pairwise interaction scores fundamentally conflate uniqueness (U), redundancy (R), and synergy (S). We prove this on a minimal 3-way XOR structural causal model: faithful indices such as Shapley-Taylor return zero per pair, whereas projective indices such as Shapley Interaction spread the third-order effect into pair scalars that conflate the three mechanisms. We introduce Stochastic Hi-Fi, a post-hoc, retraining-free predictability decomposition that estimates per-feature U/R/S profiles by interventional masked inference. The estimator provides exact interventional semantics, finite-sample Monte Carlo bounds, strict variance reduction from coupled diamond sampling, and uniform finite-vocabulary convergence. Across tabular SCMs, Stochastic Hi-Fi recovers structure missed by scalar baselines (up to 411x larger interaction-magnitude recovery ratios). It also separates redundant and synergistic heads in the GPT-2 IOI circuit. On NIH ChestX-ray14, Stochastic Hi-Fi matches GradCAM on Pointing Game and improves substantially on Deletion AUC.

</details>


### [471] [A Solver-Free Training Method for Predict-then-Optimize](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19587)
*Beichen Wan,Mo Liu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a scalable training method for machine learning models used in optimization tasks, avoiding the need for repeated optimization solver calls during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the intractability and scalability issues in training models used in the predict-then-optimize framework by minimizing decision regret efficiently.

Method: The authors propose a new decision-focused learning method using a measure transformation principle. This approach creates a surrogate loss function that eliminates the need for optimization solver calls during training.

Result: The method achieves competitive decision quality compared to state-of-the-art methods while significantly reducing training time.

Conclusion: The proposed approach demonstrates theoretical guarantees and empirically achieves scalability and effectiveness, making it a practical solution for predict-then-optimize tasks.

Abstract: We propose a scalable method for training prediction (machine learning) models in the predict-then-optimize paradigm, where model outputs serve as coefficients for a subsequent linear optimization task. Directly minimizing the empirical decision regret is intractable for linear programming and combinatorial optimization since the decision mapping is piecewise constant, and the gradients are zero almost everywhere. While existing methods address this by smoothing the differentiation process, they suffer from scalability issues, since a computationally expensive solver call is required for every gradient evaluation. To address this, we propose a decision-focused learning pipeline based on a measure transformation principle, which yields a new surrogate loss that is completely optimization-solver-free during training. We establish theoretical guarantees, including Fisher consistency and excess risk bounds. Empirically, our method achieves decision quality competitive with state-of-the-art methods while reducing training time by orders of magnitude.

</details>


### [472] [Variational Consensus Monte Carlo for Bayesian Mixture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19643)
*Julie Fendler,Francesca L. Crowe,Tom Marshall,Sylvia Richardson,Paul D. W. Kirk*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a federated learning pipeline for Bayesian mixture model inference using a Consensus Monte Carlo approach to handle privacy and sharing limitations of health data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenges posed by privacy constraints, sensitivity, and sharing limitations of health data in building accurate Bayesian mixture models.

Method: Consensus Monte Carlo (CMC) is used where MCMC algorithms are run independently in data silos and local posterior distributions are aggregated. Variational CMC is extended for over-fitted models to infer the number of clusters and model parameters without conjugacy.

Result: Simulation studies and comparisons to alternative federated learning approaches show better recovery of small data clusters when local datasets align with the underlying clustering structure.

Conclusion: The methodology reliably clusters sensitive data across distributed nodes, demonstrating its potential in applications like electronic health records for health pattern identification.

Abstract: Motivated by the privacy, sensitivity and sharing limitations of health data, we present a comprehensive pipeline for inference of Bayesian mixture models within a federated learning setting, i.e. when data cannot be fully shared or pooled across compute nodes. We adopt a Consensus Monte Carlo (CMC) approach, in which an MCMC algorithm is run independently within each data silo to estimate local posterior distributions, which are then aggregated to approximate the posterior over the full data. The variational CMC approach of Rabinovich, Angelino and Jordan (2015) [1] frames the aggregation step as a variational inference problem, but their application to mixtures assumes the number of clusters and key mixture parameters to be known. Our main methodological contributions are: (i) an extension of variational CMC to over-fitted Bayesian mixture models that infer the number of clusters and all model parameters, without requiring conjugacy; (ii) novel cluster-matching algorithms suitable for cross-silo settings in which not every cluster appears in each local dataset; (iii) a number of inference strategies for the aggregation step, matched to different federated learning constraints; and (iv) guidelines for choosing among these in practice. A comprehensive simulation study validates the framework and allows us to compare to state-of-the-art federated learning alternatives. Notably, we show that when the composition of local datasets reflects the underlying clustering structure in the data, our approach can recover small clusters with greater accuracy than standard MCMC applied to the pooled data. We illustrate the framework on large-scale electronic health record data, identifying multi-morbidity patterns in a British geriatric population.

</details>


### [473] [AURA: Adaptive Uncertainty-aware Refinement for LLM-as-a-Judge Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19714)
*Zilong Zhang,Yi-Ting Hung,Weiyi He,Junxi Zhang,Lei Ding,Chi-Kuang Yeh*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces AURA, a framework for refining LLM judgments by iteratively learning human-consistent signals and prioritizing uncertain comparisons for human review.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM judgment approaches assume reliable subsets of examples are predefined, but these assumptions are fragile due to biases and scarce human input.

Method: AURA employs an iterative process to learn human-consistency signals, propagate evidence, and prioritize uncertain comparisons for human review, treating judge trust as a latent quantity.

Result: AURA demonstrates stable refinement and effective auditing through evaluations on both synthetic and real LLM-answer data.

Conclusion: The AURA framework enhances the reliability of LLM evaluations by bridging the gap between machine and human judgment through adaptive refinement.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as judges for open-ended generation, as large-scale human evaluation is often expensive and difficult to scale, yet their preferences remain imperfect proxies for human judgment. Existing auditing pipelines often assume that a reliable subset of examples or clean supervision signals are available beforehand, for example from human annotation, heuristic filtering, or the outputs of strong judges. In LLM evaluation, this assumption is fragile: the initial split may inherit judge bias, while human verification is typically too scarce to define stable groups at scale. We propose AURA, an adaptive uncertainty--aware refinement framework for auditing pairwise LLM--as--a--judge decisions under selected human verification. AURA iteratively learns a human-consistency signal, propagates reliable evidence, and prioritizes uncertain comparisons for human review. The key idea is to treat trust in a judge as a latent quantity that is progressively refined as evidence accumulates. We provide a compact formulation, a stable refinement procedure, and a comprehensive evaluation on both synthetic and real pairwise LLM-answer data.

</details>


### [474] [Stochastic Linear Contextual Bandits with Bounded Noise: A Set-Membership Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20022)
*Haonan Xu,Yingying Li*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents a new algorithm, SME-OFU, for stochastic linear contextual bandits with bounded reward noise, achieving improved regret bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation in existing works on stochastic linear contextual bandits that assume sub-Gaussian reward noise and do not explicitly leverage the more informative bounded reward noise.

Method: A novel algorithm, SME-OFU, is proposed using set-membership estimation (SME) for uncertainty quantification and applying optimism in the face of uncertainty (OFU) principle.

Result: SME-OFU achieves an improved regret bound of $O(\log T)$, outperforming the typical $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ bound under bounded noise conditions. Simulations demonstrate its empirical superiority over benchmarks for sub-Gaussian noise.

Conclusion: Explicitly leveraging bounded reward noise leads to significant improvements in regret performance, offering theoretical and empirical advancements for contextual bandit frameworks.

Abstract: This paper considers stochastic linear contextual bandits (SLCB) with bounded reward noise. Existing works typically assume sub-Gaussian reward noise and bounded expected rewards, under which the optimal regret bound scales as $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ in terms of horizon $T$. However, in many applications, realized/observed rewards are also naturally bounded, implying bounded reward noise. Bounded noise is more informative than the sub-Gaussian condition but has not been leveraged explicitly in the SLCB literature. In this paper, we propose a novel algorithm SME-OFU by utilizing an uncertainty quantification method called set-membership estimation (SME) and applying the principle of optimism in the face of uncertainty (OFU). Our algorithm enjoys an improved regret bound $O(\log T)$. Notice that this does not contradict the existing optimal bound $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ for sub-Gaussian noise because bounded noise is a stronger condition. Finally, simulations show empirical improvements of SME-OFU over a benchmark algorithm designed for sub-Gaussian noise when the reward noise is bounded.

</details>


### [475] [AK-MCS-C2 : Active Kriging Monte Carlo Simulation method with conformal certification for failure probability estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20191)
*Edgar Jaber,Vincent Chabridon,Mathilde Mougeot*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel framework combining Active Kriging Monte Carlo simulation and conformal prediction to improve failure probability estimation in structural reliability analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for more reliable and robust methods for failure probability estimation in small-sample, rare-event scenarios, which standard approaches struggle with.

Method: The approach integrates Active Kriging Monte Carlo simulation with an adaptive cross-conformal strategy using the J+GP conformal estimator for uncertainty quantification.

Result: The new method demonstrated improved prediction accuracy, reliability, and robustness in estimating failure probabilities, especially for rare-event regimes. Numerical results validated its effectiveness compared to classical methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enhances reliability and efficiency in structural reliability analysis, offering distribution-free prediction guarantees and superior performance in challenging scenarios.

Abstract: We introduce a novel active-learning framework for failure probability estimation in structural reliability analysis that integrates Active Kriging Monte Carlo simulation with conformal prediction. The proposed approach employs an adaptive cross-conformal strategy specifically designed for small-sample settings and kriging surrogate models using the J+GP conformal estimator. Unlike standard AK-MCS methods, the proposed framework provides distribution-free guarantees on prediction errors, leading to more reliable classification of samples near the limit-state surface. This improved uncertainty quantification enhances both the accuracy and robustness of failure probability estimates, especially for rare-event regimes where such efficiency is crucial. Reproducible numerical results illustrate the effectiveness of the method and also compare it to classical approaches on well-established benchmarks.

</details>


### [476] [Off-Policy Evaluation for Missingness-Aware Policies in MDPs with Rewards Missing Not at Random](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20206)
*Ziheng Wei,Annie Qu,Rui Miao*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper tackles off-policy evaluation in Reinforcement Learning when rewards in the dataset are missing not at random (MNAR), proposing methods to address induced selection bias while ensuring strong experimental performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge in offline reinforcement learning where logged batch data often contains missing or censored rewards, which complicates evaluation tasks. This problem is common in practical contexts like healthcare and marketing.

Method: The authors formalize a reward-dependent propensity model and introduce a bridge function to recover conditional mean rewards without needing to explicitly model MNAR mechanisms. They estimate this using a min-max procedure and build an evaluation method that incorporates recovered rewards and accounts for missingness.

Result: The paper provides mathematical guarantees, including consistency and finite-sample error bounds for the proposed estimator. Experimental results on simulated and MIMIC-III Sepsis data demonstrate strong performance compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed methodology effectively handles MNAR reward situations in offline reinforcement learning, offering both theoretical robustness and practical utility as shown through experiments.

Abstract: In offline Reinforcement Learning, immediate rewards in logged batch data are often unobserved due to sparse or irregular record-keeping, or censored beyond certain reward values. This issue arises in practical settings, including health care and marketing. We investigate off-policy evaluation (OPE) in finite-horizon Markov decision processes when rewards are missing not at random (MNAR), which breaks ignorability and induces selection bias even after conditioning on states and actions. To address this, we formalize a reward-dependent propensity model and use future states as shadow variables to identify the full-data conditional mean reward. We further introduce a bridge function that recovers the conditional mean reward without explicitly modeling the MNAR mechanism, and estimate it via a min-max procedure to avoid double sampling. Building upon these identification results, we propose an Fitted-Q-Evaluation-style estimator that propagates the recovered rewards while allowing target policies to depend on past missingness indicators. Finally, we establish consistency and finite-sample error bounds for our OPE estimator, and show through experiments the strong performance of our method compared to existing methods on simulated and MIMIC-III Sepsis data.

</details>


### [477] [Statistical Properties of Training & Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20299)
*Itay Lavie,Noam Levi,Yonatan Kahn*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper explores deep learning from a physics-informed perspective, focusing on neural scaling laws and inductive biases in physics applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand deep learning features and surprises highlighting how they achieve unprecedented performance, with a specific focus on applying deep learning to physics problems.

Method: The paper reviews neural scaling laws and analyzes how constraints and inductive biases influence deep learning applied to physics tasks.

Result: The investigation provides insights into how scaling laws and biases interact and shape physics-informed deep learning models.

Conclusion: Physics-informed perspectives and neural scaling laws play significant roles in understanding and optimizing deep learning for solving real-world physics tasks.

Abstract: Deep learning has managed to evade numerous intuitions from classical statistics to achieve unprecedented performance on a number of real-world tasks. In this article, we investigate the key features and surprises of deep learning from a physics-informed perspective, taking care to point out and justify where possible the many choices inherent in constructing a deep learning model. In particular, we review the phenomenon of neural scaling laws and discuss their interplay with the constraints and inductive biases which may be present when applying machine learning to problems in physics.

</details>


### [478] [SSH-Net: A Deep Neural Network for Predicting Failure Time Distribution Functions under Competing Risks with Application to GPU Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20451)
*Jie Min,Yueyao Wang,Mengkun Chen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces SSH-Net, a deep learning model for failure time prediction in the context of competing risks, addressing challenges in hyperparameter tuning and hierarchical data representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in time-to-event modeling of competing risks in engineering, particularly the limitations of deep neural networks in hyperparameter tuning and capturing critical information in hierarchical data structures.

Method: The proposed SSH-Net uses separate subnetworks for different covariate groups within a cause-specific competing risks framework. It outputs cause-specific hazard functions and utilizes penalized log-likelihood as the loss function.

Result: The SSH-Net's prediction accuracy is validated through simulations and evaluation metrics like Brier score, AUC, and RMSE, along with a real-world application on Titan GPU failure time data.

Conclusion: SSH-Net improves failure time predictions under competing risks, providing a robust approach to handle hierarchical data structures and diverse input groups.

Abstract: Competing risks are commonly observed in engineering fields and can bring challenges to time-to-event data modeling when the application scenarios are complicated. Recently, deep neural networks have received great attention for prediction with competing risks, due to their flexibility and high learning capability. However, the complexity of neural network structure brings extra difficulty in hyperparameter tuning based on different data inputs. Additionally, when an engineered system has complex physical structures with multiple hierarchical levels, treating all structural levels as a single group of inputs may fail to capture critical information. To address the issues, we propose a Structured Segmented Hazard Deep Neural Network (SSH-Net) for failure time prediction under cause-specific competing risks framework. Our approach associates neural network structure with data structures, and allows different covariate groups to impact the failure prediction through separate sub-networks. The neural network is constructed based on a cause-specific competing risks model. The SSH-Net outputs cause-specific hazard functions, and utilizes the penalized log-likelihood as the loss function. The prediction accuracy of SSH-Net is validated through simulation studies by evaluating the Brier score, the area under receiver operating characteristic curves (AUC), and the root mean square error (RMSE) of the predicted cause-specific cumulative incident function. We further demonstrate the model's ability to predict failure time distribution functions using the Titan GPU failure time data.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [479] [CoarseSolvers for Exascale Solution of Poisson Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20496)
*Thilina Ratnayaka,Paul Fischer,Luke Olson*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper proposes a two-level Schwarz method as an alternative to AMG for solving pressure Poisson equations, focusing on effectiveness and structured coarse spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the efficiency of solving pressure Poisson equations in incompressible Navier-Stokes, especially addressing limitations of current AMG solvers in the p-multigrid context.

Method: A novel, structured, and non-nested coarse space is developed for a two-level Schwarz method, enabling communication-free interpolation and tested on high-performance computing systems using Nek5000/RS.

Result: Experiments on Summit/Frontier supercomputers show improved efficiency and effectiveness of the proposed method over the state-of-the-art AMG solver BoomerAMG.

Conclusion: The structured two-level Schwarz method demonstrates better performance than existing AMG approaches, making it a robust alternative for pressure Poisson equations in computational fluid dynamics.

Abstract: WepresentatwolevelSchwarzmethodasanalternativetoAlgebraicMultigridmethod(AMG) used as the last level (coarse) solver of the p-multigrid pMG preconditioner for pressure Poission equation resulting from Spectral/Finite element descretization of incompressible Navier-Stokes eqaution. Proposed Schwarz method consits of a local problem in the original pMG coarse space and a global coarse problem. Main contribution of the paper is a novel, structured and a non-nested coarse space for the global coarse problem. Structured nature of the proposed global coarse space enable communication-free interpolation between the original p-multgrid coarse space and the global coarse problem. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared to the state of the art AMG solver BoomerAMG by a series of experiments performed using Nek5000/RS, a suite of highly scalable incompressible Navier-Stokes solvers, on Summit/Frontier supercomputers at Oak Ridge Leadership Computing Facility.

</details>


### [480] [Neural Architectures as Functional Priors in Physics-Informed Control Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19368)
*Sonia Rubio Herranz,Fernando Carlos López Hernández,Antonio López Montes*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: This study investigates how neural architectures act as implicit priors in control problems using ordinary differential equations, focusing on simple controlled dynamical systems. Numerical experiments reveal that different neural architectures lead to distinct control solutions with variations in smoothness, energy distribution, and oscillatory content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the impact of neural network architectures on the controls of dynamical systems by systematically investigating how these architectures influence the learned solutions.

Method: The authors analyze controlled linear RLC circuits and nonlinear Duffing systems using both classical optimal-control methods and PINN-based approaches. They compare multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and Fourier-based architectures to analyze their effect on control trajectories.

Result: The study finds that neural architecture choice significantly affects the control outputs, with Fourier-based architectures yielding oscillatory trajectories and MLPs producing smoother, energy-efficient controls.

Conclusion: Neural architectures implicitly influence the functional specialization of control systems. Optimal control problems may benefit from selecting architectures that align with the nature of the specific functional components of the control problem.

Abstract: In this work we investigate the role of neural architectures as implicit functional priors in control problems governed by ordinary differential equations. Rather than focusing on highly complex problems, our objective is to investigate architecture-dependent effects in controlled dynamical systems within the simplest physically interpretable settings possible. In particular, we study a controlled linear RLC electrical circuit and a nonlinear Duffing-type dynamical system. Both systems are analyzed first through classical optimal-control formulations and later through PINN-based approaches. We compare different combinations of multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and Fourier-based KAN-like architectures, and analyze their influence on the resulting controls. The numerical experiments suggest that different architectural choices systematically generate qualitatively distinct controls, even under identical governing equations, loss functionals, initial and target states, training parameters and physical constraints. Significant differences appear in the spectral structure, smoothness, energy distribution, and phase-space behavior of the learned solutions. A central observation of this work is the emergence of a functional specialization phenomenon when the neural architectures are allowed sufficient freedom to shape the structure of the learned controls. More specifically, in the systems considered here, Fourier-based architectures tend to produce trajectories with richer oscillatory content, whereas smoother low-frequency-biased architectures tend to generate more regular and energetically efficient controls. This suggests that different functional components of the control problem may be handled more efficiently by different neural architectures, leading to an implicit specialization between state representation and control generation.

</details>


### [481] [A fast direct solver based neural network for solving PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19895)
*Jashwanth Reddy Kadaru,Vaishnavi Gujjula*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper develops neural network architectures to learn and solve problems involving Hierarchical Off-Diagonal Low-Rank matrices, extending application to nonlinear PDE operators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently solving problems arising in large-scale $N$-body computations and nonlinear PDEs using hierarchical matrices requires novel methods that integrate deep learning with fast direct solvers.

Method: The authors introduce a neural network inspired by the existing fast solver for HODLR matrices. The architecture is expanded to handle nonlinear PDEs by incorporating deep sub-networks within linear layers.

Result: Experiments demonstrate improved performance and efficiency across various problems, including integral equations and nonlinear PDE solving, with generalization and faster inference times compared to classical numerical methods.

Conclusion: The proposed network offers a promising alternative to traditional solvers for both linear and nonlinear problems, leveraging hierarchical matrix structures within neural frameworks.

Abstract: The matrices arising from large scale $N$-body problems can be efficiently represented using hierarchical matrices, whose key idea is that the admissible off-diagonal sub-matrices can be well approximated by low-rank matrices across a hierarchy of matrix partitions. HODLR (Hierarchical Off-Diagonal Low-Rank) matrices are a subclass of hierarchical matrices in which all off-diagonal submatrices at every level of a recursive binary partition are low-rank. In this article, we present a neural network that learns the inverse operation of HODLR matrices based on the fast direct solver for HODLR matrices developed by Ambikasaran and Darve (2013). We further extend the architecture to learn nonlinear solution operators associated with PDEs by replacing some of the linear layers with deep sub-networks. We demonstrate the performance of the proposed architecture by performing a comprehensive set of experiments that include (i) solving a linear problem such as the Fredholm integral equation of the second kind, (ii) solving PDEs such as the nonlinear Schrödinger equation, Burgers' equation, and the steady-state Darcy's flow equation, (iii) generalization study across varying parameter values, (iv) comparing the inference time of the proposed network with the run time of a classical numerical solver, and (v) comparing the proposed network with some of the existing neural operator learning networks.

</details>


### [482] [Structure-Oriented Randomized Neural Networks for Poisson-Nernst-Planck and Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Navier-Stokes Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19912)
*Yunlong Li,Fei Wang*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper introduces a randomized neural network framework for solving PNP and PNP-NS systems, focusing on structure preservation, mass conservation, and positivity enforcement. Numerical tests validate its effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The primary motivation is to address challenges in solving the coupled Poisson-Nernst-Planck and Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Navier-Stokes systems, especially ensuring structural integrity, mass conservation, positivity of concentration variables, and satisfying physical constraints like incompressibility.

Method: The authors propose the SO-RaNN framework that uses randomized neural networks in a space-time setup to iteratively solve decoupled linearized subproblems. Techniques like pointwise cut-off for positivity, mass-correction factors, and SAV-type post-processing are incorporated to enhance performance. For the velocity field in the PNP-NS system, the SP-RaNN approach ensures divergence-free approximation.

Result: Theoretical results include residual-based error estimates for linearized subproblems, conditional convergence proofs, and error analysis. Numerical experiments validate the framework's approximation accuracy, mass preservation, positivity enforcement, and incompressibility adherence.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that SO-RaNN and SP-RaNN frameworks effectively solve PNP and PNP-NS systems while maintaining their structural properties. The additional corrections and post-processing steps ensure consistency with physical principles.

Abstract: We develop a structure-oriented randomized neural network framework, termed SO-RaNN, for the Poisson-Nernst-Planck (PNP) system and the Poisson-Nernst-Planck-Navier-Stokes (PNP-NS) system. The decoupled linearized subproblems are solved iteratively by randomized neural networks in a space-time framework. For the concentration variables, a pointwise cut-off is used to enforce positivity at the value level, and discrete mass-scaling factors are computed at selected correction instants and interpolated in time, so as to ensure exact mass matching at those instants and to promote approximate mass preservation between them. To introduce an auxiliary discrete dissipation mechanism, we further employ an SAV-type post-processing correction, which yields monotonicity of the SAV auxiliary variable under the ideal SAV update. For the PNP-NS system, a structure-preserving randomized neural network (SP-RaNN) is used for the velocity field, so that the velocity approximation satisfies the incompressibility constraint pointwise by construction. On the theoretical side, we derive residual-based estimates for the raw, uncorrected RaNN solvers of the linearized subproblems, formulate a conditional local-in-time convergence result for the raw outer Picard iteration of the PNP system, and analyze the value-level positivity correction together with the mass-correction and SAV post-processing steps. For the PNP-NS system, we establish an approximation result for the SP-RaNN space and provide a conditional error statement for the corresponding linearized Oseen-type problem. Numerical experiments demonstrate approximation accuracy in the source-driven manufactured tests and illustrate the intended value-level positivity correction, selected-time mass matching, computed free-energy curves based on the final gauge-fixed potential, and divergence-free approximation in benchmark tests.

</details>


<div id='hep-ex'></div>

# hep-ex [[Back]](#toc)

### [483] [Towards Engineering Scaling Laws with Pretraining Data Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19781)
*Jan-Lucas Uslu,Kevin Greif,Daniel Whiteson,Benjamin Nachman*

Main category: hep-ex

TL;DR: This paper explores how neural scaling laws apply to particle physics models and demonstrates the impact of pretraining with diverse synthetic data on performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how neural scaling laws extend to particle physics, leveraging high-fidelity simulators to optimize model performance and resource usage.

Method: Pretrained models on synthetic data generated from high-fidelity simulators with an emphasis on diversity and alignment with the classification task of hadronic jets.

Result: Scaling laws in particle physics can be influenced by pretraining with diverse and task-specific data, leading to a regime favoring larger datasets over larger models.

Conclusion: Synthetic data diversity and alignment are pivotal in optimizing neural scaling for particle physics, redirecting reliance from increasing model size to increasing dataset size.

Abstract: Neural scaling laws describe how model performance improves as a power law in compute, model size, and dataset size. While well-established for large language models, these relationships are emerging for large models in particle physics. As with language, empirical studies show that the performance scales as a power law. However, unlike natural language or image domains, fundamental physics has high-fidelity simulators that produce synthetic data cheaply. This favors scaling regimes where additional data is cheaper than additional parameters, and allows the pretraining dataset itself to be engineered to influence the scaling. For the task of classifying hadronic jets produced in collisions of high-energy particle beams, we show that the scaling behavior can be engineered towards requiring more data rather than larger models by inclusion of pretraining data which is more diverse and better aligned with the downstream classification task.

</details>


### [484] [HEPTv2: End-to-End Efficient Point Transformer for Charged Particle Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20437)
*Siqi Miao,Shitij Govil,Jack P. Rodgers,Mia Liu,Javier Duarte,Shih-Chieh Hsu,Yuan-Tang Chou,Pan Li*

Main category: hep-ex

TL;DR: The paper introduces HEPTv2, an end-to-end transformer architecture for charged-particle tracking that improves efficiency and reduces computational costs, surpassing existing models and meeting the demands of HL-LHC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing collision densities at the HL-LHC require accurate and efficient charged-particle tracking methods. Current methods like graph neural networks and transformers are computationally expensive or prevent end-to-end optimization, necessitating a streamlined approach.

Method: HEPTv2 is an end-to-end point-transformer architecture that eliminates graph-building and auxiliary stages. It uses a locality-aware point encoder with efficient local attention and a sectorized track decoder to enable direct hit-to-track prediction under joint supervision.

Result: HEPTv2 achieves a tracking efficiency of 98.6% with only 0.8% fake rate on the TrackML dataset, requiring ~15 ms inference time and 0.4 GB memory per event on an NVIDIA A100 GPU. Its accuracy is 4.5% better than prior transformers and 1.1--2.2% better than graph-based pipelines, with significant reductions in latency.

Conclusion: HEPTv2 sets a new standard in the accuracy-latency trade-off for particle tracking, demonstrating that end-to-end transformer architectures meet the stringent requirements of real-time particle reconstruction at the HL-LHC.

Abstract: Charged-particle tracking -- reconstructing trajectories from sparse detector measurements -- is a fundamental high-energy-physics inference problem and a canonical example of learning under extreme combinatorial ambiguity. At the High-Luminosity Large Hadron Collider (HL-LHC), tracking must remain accurate and efficient despite unprecedented collision densities. Graph neural networks perform strongly, but incur substantial costs from graph construction and processing, while transformer-based approaches rely on auxiliary stages that prevent end-to-end optimization. To address this, we present HEPTv2, an end-to-end point-transformer architecture that reconstructs tracks from detector hits in one trainable pipeline. HEPTv2 combines a locality-aware point encoder with a track decoder that predicts complete trajectories without graph-building, clustering, or filtering. The encoder uses locality-sensitive hashing in detector coordinate space to preserve tracking-relevant geometry while enabling efficient local attention. The decoder resolves ambiguities through sectorized decoding and direct hit-to-track prediction under joint encoder-decoder supervision, allowing the full pipeline to be optimized end-to-end. On TrackML, HEPTv2 achieves 98.6% double-majority tracking efficiency at a 0.8% fake rate, while requiring only $\sim$15~ms inference time and 0.4~GB peak memory per event on a NVIDIA A100 GPU. Latency and memory scale approximately linearly for events with up to $5\times10^5$ hits. HEPTv2 establishes a new state of the art in the accuracy-latency trade-off, improving efficiency by 4.5% over the strongest prior transformer and by 1.1--2.2% over optimized graph-based pipelines, while reducing latency by factors of 7 and 38--52, respectively. These results show end-to-end transformers can deliver the accuracy and efficiency required for real-time particle reconstruction at the HL-LHC.

</details>


<div id='econ.GN'></div>

# econ.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [485] [AI Economist Agent: An Agentic Framework for Model-Grounded Economic Analysis with RAG, Knowledge Graphs, and Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20041)
*Masahiro Kato*

Main category: econ.GN

TL;DR: The paper presents an AI economist framework using LLMs and knowledge graphs to produce economically coherent and traceable reports by grounding them in economic theory and real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Economists need to make grounded claims based on economic theory and real-world data, but LLMs often lack the capability to directly integrate such foundational grounding into their generated narratives.

Method: The study introduces a RAG-based AI economist framework integrating knowledge graphs and LLM-based agents to plan, retrieve evidence, select models, and generate reports. The framework emphasizes grounding narratives in explicit model computations and evidence via AI agents.

Result: The framework was tested on two applications: U.S. inflation persistence and Federal Reserve policy report generation, and bank stress-test narrative generation for commercial real estate refinancing. The results highlight improved economic coherence and traceability of generated reports.

Conclusion: Grounding AI-generated economic narratives in theory and data enhances their utility for economic analysis and decision-making.

Abstract: We propose a model-grounded RAG-based AI economist with an agentic framework for economic scenario analysis using large language models (LLMs) and knowledge graphs. While LLMs can generate fluent economic narratives, economists are often required to make economic claims grounded by economic theory and real-world data. Based on this motivation, this study proposes an RAG-based AI economist, which utilizes knowledge graphs including economic data and theory and LLM-based agents to plan the analysis, retrieve relevant evidence, select appropriate models, and generate reports. In our framework, we do not produce quantitative claims directly with the language model alone; instead, we generate narratives grounded in explicit model-based computations and linked to the retrieved evidence via AI agents. We refer to our framework as an AI economist agent. We evaluate the AI economist agent in two applications: economist report generation for U.S. inflation persistence and Federal Reserve policy, and bank stress-test narrative generation for U.S. commercial real estate refinancing stress. The results illustrate how grounding the generated reports improves their economic coherence and traceability.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [486] [REMOP: REmote-Memory-aware OPerator Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19576)
*Shiquan Zhang,Yunhao Mao,Yuqiu Zhang,Gengrui Zhang,Jeyhun Karimov,Hans-Arno Jacobsen*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces REMOP, a framework optimizing database query operators for remote memory environments to minimize transfer rounds and reduce execution time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current query processing strategies for out-of-memory workloads are suboptimal in remote memory setups as they cause excessive transfer rounds, increasing latency.

Method: REMOP incorporates transfer-round-aware latency models into operator design and applies buffer-partitioning strategies for specific operations in DuckDB.

Result: On a two-node setup, REMOP reduced transfer rounds (up to 97%), operator runtime (up to 48%), and lowered end-to-end query runtime for benchmarks by over 22%.

Conclusion: REMOP effectively addresses remote memory inefficiencies by accounting for transfer rounds, significantly enhancing query performance in constrained memory environments.

Abstract: Remote and disaggregated memory tiers expand the effective memory capacity of analytical database engines, but they also reshape the cost structure of out-of-memory query processing. When an operator spills beyond local DRAM, moving pages to remote memory incurs both data-transfer time and a fixed round-trip latency per transfer. Classical operator analyses and buffer-allocation heuristics primarily target disk spilling by minimizing total I/O volume. Under remote memory, these strategies can be suboptimal because they may trigger excessive transfer rounds. We present REMOP, a remote-memory-aware operator optimization framework that uses transfer-round-aware intra-operator memory policies to improve out-of-memory execution under tight memory budgets. REMOP introduces the number of transfer rounds into the latency cost model and derives operator-specific buffer-partitioning strategies, instantiating the approach for blocked nested-loop join, external merge sort, and external hash join in DuckDB. Our evaluation on a two-node compute-memory testbed shows that REMOP reduces transfer rounds by up to 97% and operator runtime by up to 48% on spill-heavy microbenchmarks, and lowers the average runtime of spilling TPC-H and TPC-DS queries by 22.7% and 26.4% end-to-end.

</details>


### [487] [The Bi-Channel Networking Paradigm for Database Systems in the Cloud](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19969)
*Georg Kreuzmayr,Muhammad El-Hindi,Benjamin Wagner,Tobias Ziegler,Viktor Leis*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces the bi-channel paradigm to address CPU overhead issues in cloud databases by separating data and control communication paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Network links have become faster, but traditional kernel TCP stacks cause high CPU overhead, limiting database performance.

Method: The authors propose a bi-channel paradigm, using user-space UDP for performance-intensive tasks and kernel TCP for control operations, leveraging modern NIC capabilities.

Result: Implemented in two settings, the paradigm achieved 200 Gbit/s with 3 CPU cores and processed millions of messages per second in a key-value store.

Conclusion: Co-designing networking with database operations via the bi-channel paradigm significantly enhances performance and scalability.

Abstract: When network links were slow, cloud and distributed database systems could rely on generic kernel abstractions and treat network communication as a black box. With today's fast cloud networks, this approach breaks down: database performance becomes limited by the CPU overhead of the kernel TCP stack. Replacing TCP with user-space UDP can reduce this overhead, but it requires reimplementing essential guarantees, such as reliability and ordering. To solve this conundrum, database systems should no longer treat networking as a black box but co-design it with database operations. We propose the bi-channel paradigm for database systems, which separates communication into two channels: A high-performance data path for latency- and bandwidth-sensitive operations, and a reliable control path for coordination and recovery. We implement the paradigm by combining user-space UDP and kernel-based TCP, though other stack combinations are possible. This design exploits modern NIC capabilities while preserving TCP's reliability. We demonstrate the paradigm's efficiency and simplicity in two representative settings: a distributed shuffle saturating 200 Gbit/s with three CPU cores, and a replicated key-value store processing millions of messages per second.

</details>


### [488] [Policy-aware Vector Search: A Vision for Fine Grained Access Control in Vector Databases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19803)
*Lakshmi Sahithi Yalamarthi,Primal Pappachan*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: This paper addresses the limited security capabilities of vector databases, particularly in implementing Fine-grained Access Control (FGAC), and proposes a vision for policy-aware vector search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vector databases are increasingly used in security-sensitive AI applications, which require robust FGAC policies to ensure user-specific data access control, a functionality currently underdeveloped in such databases.

Method: The paper formalizes FGAC policy models and the enforcement problem for vector databases, compares enforcement strategies, and reports preliminary findings.

Result: Key enforcement strategies were compared, and preliminary insights were provided, though several open challenges were identified for future research.

Conclusion: Fine-grained Access Control in vector databases presents notable challenges involving ANN search recall and query latency. A formalized FGAC model and initial findings offer groundwork for advancing policy-aware vector search.

Abstract: Vector databases are increasingly used in security sensitive contexts with Retrieval Augmented Generation and organizational AI pipelines; however, their security capabilities remain limited. Specifically, Fine-grained Access Control (FGAC) which is required to ensure that data access adheres to user-specific policies is not fully supported in modern vector databases. Unlike relational databases, vector databases combine structured and unstructured attributes to provide semantic, approximate query results, which complicates FGAC implementation. This creates an inherent tension between enforcing FGAC policies correctly, achieving high ANN search recall and maintaining low query latency. In this paper, we present a vision for Policy-aware Vector Search by formalizing the FGAC policy model in vector databases as well as the enforcement problem. We compare various enforcement strategies, present preliminary findings, and identify key open challenges for future research in policy-aware vector search.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [489] [Closing the Calibration Gap in Semantic Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19719)
*Aditeya Baral,Radoslav Ralev,Iliya Sotirov Zhechev,Srijith Rajamohan,Jen Agarwal*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper introduces improved metrics for semantic caching in LLM inference systems, addressing deployment issues caused by current evaluation practices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation metrics like PR-AUC fail to align with operational needs in semantic caching systems, leading to suboptimal deployment choices.

Method: Proposed metrics include P-CHR AUC to measure precision across cache levels and CRR to quantify offline-to-deployment quality retention. They also analyze operational gaps by decomposing calibration and structural issues.

Result: Experiments reveal the calibration gap is influenced by training objectives rather than data size, and post-hoc calibration is only partially effective in mitigating it.

Conclusion: Semantic caching system evaluation should prioritize calibration-focused metrics instead of ranking-focused ones to improve deployment effectiveness.

Abstract: Semantic caching cuts LLM inference costs by serving a cached response to semantically similar queries. Standard practice evaluates these systems using PR-AUC, a metric that only measures how well scores rank and ignores whether they are usable at a fixed threshold. We show this mismatch leads to systematically poor deployment choices, as models with the highest PR-AUC are often the worst in operation. We introduce Precision-Cache Hit Ratio (P-CHR) AUC, a cache-aware metric that measures precision across cache utilization levels, and Calibration Retention Rate (CRR), which captures how much offline ranking quality survives at deployment. We decompose the operational gap between offline and deployed quality into a recoverable calibration component and an irreducible structural component fixed by the dataset's positive rate. Our experiments show that the calibration gap is governed by the training objective rather than data scale, and post-hoc calibration only partially closes it. Ultimately, model selection for semantic caching is a calibration problem, not a ranking one, and measuring it is the first step to closing the gap.

</details>


### [490] [Generative Engine Optimization at Scale: Measuring Brand Visibility Across AI Search Engines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20065)
*Pratyush Kumar*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper examines how brands can optimize for AI search engine visibility (GEO) by analyzing 100,000+ responses across 100+ brands, revealing disparities in visibility based on brand maturity and proposing protocols for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to help brands adapt to the rise of AI search engines and optimize their visibility, given the shift from traditional search engine optimization (SEO) to AI-focused approaches like Generative Engine Optimization (GEO).

Method: The authors analyzed over 100,000 responses across 100+ brands on AI platforms between March and May 2026 to explore visibility trends, citation preferences, and sentiment patterns.

Result: The research identified a brand-stature ladder where global brands dominate visibility (73%), followed by mid-market brands (44%) and small/niche brands (11%). It also showed that corporate websites and "best-of" listicles are the most-cited sources, while sentiment framing proves unstable.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a baseline for measuring AI brand visibility, demonstrating platform differences and maturity disparities, and suggests protocols for improving AI search visibility.

Abstract: People increasingly get answers straight from AI search engines like ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Gemini rather than scrolling search results. Brands that once focused on search engine optimization (SEO) must now optimize for how these engines represent, cite, and recommend them -- a shift variously called Generative Engine Optimization (GEO), Answer Engine Optimization (AEO), and AI Search Visibility. We treat AEO and AI Visibility as part of GEO, and study how to measure brand visibility across AI engines: what they value when they cite a brand, which sources they rely on, and what content large language models surface. The hard case is everyone outside the already-authoritative top brands -- SMEs, D2C brands, creators, and early-stage startups.
  We analyze 100K+ prompt responses across 100+ brands tracked on Ranqo between March and May 2026. First visibility runs form a clear three-tier brand-stature ladder: global household names (e.g., Stripe, Nike) appear in 73% of relevant AI answers on their first run; established mid-market and regional brands (e.g., Olipop, Klaviyo) in 44%; niche and small brands in just 11% -- about 30 percentage points per step. When engines cite sources, about 78% go to corporate websites; among non-corporate sources YouTube leads, ahead of Reddit, editorial media, and Wikipedia. The highest-leverage page is the ranked "best-of" listicle, the most-cited content format at about 21% of all citations. Sentiment is the unstable signal: whether a brand is framed positively or negatively flips about 6.7 times more often than whether it is mentioned at all.
  These findings provide a first large-scale baseline for measuring GEO: AI brand visibility can be measured, differs by platform, and varies strongly by brand maturity. We close by proposing seven v1.1 protocols to test whether specific recommendations can causally improve AI visibility.

</details>


### [491] [SAFE-Cascade: Cost-Adaptive Vision-Language Routing for Chart Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19646)
*Ayush Dwivedi,Qixin Wang,Ashvi Soni,Ruoteng Wang,Han Li,Animesh Mahapatra,Neeraj Agrawal,Xintao Wu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces SAFE-Cascade, a system for cost-efficient chart question answering, which combines OCR and text-only models with visual-language models (VLMs) selectively and adaptively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs are resource-intensive to use for every chart question, even when many answers can be derived using simpler OCR and text-based reasoning. The motivation is to reduce unnecessary VLM usage while retaining performance.

Method: SAFE-Cascade extracts text data using OCR, generates provisional answers via a text-only language model, and uses a Random Forest-based router to decide whether to invoke a VLM. The interactive system allows users to adjust decision parameters and explore trade-offs.

Result: SAFE-Cascade reduces VLM calls by 26.9% while achieving comparable accuracy (69.1%) to a full-VLM baseline (67.7% accuracy with 100% VLM calls), leading to a 9.3% reduction in cost.

Conclusion: Selective modality routing, as demonstrated by SAFE-Cascade, is a viable approach to balancing accuracy, cost, and transparency in multimodal systems. It highlights the potential for tunable, cost-efficient solutions in AI-driven tasks.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are powerful for chart question answering, but invoking a VLM for every query can be unnecessarily expensive when many questions are answerable from OCR text and lightweight language reasoning. We demonstrate SAFE-Cascade, an interactive system for cost-adaptive chart question answering. Given a chart image and a natural-language question, SAFE-Cascade first extracts chart text with OCR, obtains a provisional answer from a text-only language model, and then uses a learned router to decide whether to accept the text answer or escalate to a VLM. The demo exposes this decision process to users: OCR evidence, text-only answer, routing probability, escalation decision, final answer, estimated cost, and estimated latency are shown side by side. SAFE-Cascade is designed as a transparent interface for understanding when visual grounding is actually needed. Users can upload or select charts, ask questions, inspect the evidence used by each pathway, compare text-only and VLM answers, and adjust the escalation threshold to explore the accuracy-cost frontier. The system is implemented with Azure Document Intelligence for OCR, gpt-5-mini as the text-only model, gemini-2.5-flash-image as the VLM, and a Random Forest router trained on inference-time features. On a held-out ChartQA test split of 375 examples from a 2,500-example experiment, SAFE-Cascade achieves 69.1% unified accuracy with 73.1% VLM invocation, compared with 67.7% accuracy and 100% VLM invocation for the full-VLM baseline. The observed +1.4 percentage-point difference is statistically uncertain, so we interpret SAFE-Cascade as matching full-VLM performance while reducing VLM calls by 26.9% and estimated cost by 9.3%. The demonstration shows how selective modality routing can make multimodal knowledge systems more transparent, tunable, and cost-aware.

</details>


### [492] [VCG: A Multimodal Retrieval Framework for E-Commerce Video Feeds under Extreme Cold-Start Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19627)
*Katya Mirylenka,Egor Malykh,Mahdyar Ravanbakhsh,Michael Gygli,Marco-Andrea Buchmann,Andrew Dzhoha,Svitlana Borzenko,Francesca Catino,Mohamed Gaafar,Maarten Versteegh,Thomas Kober,Dario d'Andrea,Ellie Langhans*

Main category: cs.IR
